meta.title: Big Idea 5 Exam — Impact of Computing

partI.instructions: Choose the single best answer. Several items combine ideas from more than one topic — read each scenario fully before answering.

questions.stem: Earbuds translate 40 languages in real time. Tour guides celebrate that travelers can chat with vendors anywhere; language teachers report collapsing enrollment. Both react to the SAME effect. This BEST illustrates which idea?
options: Every effect of a computing innovation is anticipated by its creators before release
Harmful effects occur only when an innovation is used exactly as its creators intended
Computing innovations rarely change the way people complete everyday tasks
A single effect of a computing innovation can be viewed as beneficial by some people and harmful by others
questions.rationale: IOC-1.A.4 says a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people — the same translation effect delights guides and alarms teachers. A contradicts IOC-1.A.3 (not every effect is anticipated in advance). B is wrong — the teachers' complaint concerns the intended use itself, and harm does not require misuse (IOC-1.B.2). C contradicts IOC-1.A.2: the way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate new innovations, which is exactly what both groups observe.

questions.stem: A studio's racing-game physics engine is later adapted by engineers to simulate stadium evacuations — a use the studio never considered. Which statement best describes this outcome?
options: A harmful impact on society produced by the rapid sharing of a program
An unintended beneficial effect, in which a computing innovation led to advances in another field
Evidence that responsible programmers can anticipate every future use of their innovations
The digital divide, since only credentialed engineers were given access to the engine
questions.rationale: IOC-1.B.5 says computing innovations have often had unintended beneficial effects by leading to advances in other fields, and IOC-1.B.1 says innovations can be used in ways their creators had not originally intended — a game engine advancing public-safety engineering fits both. A mislabels a clearly beneficial outcome as harmful. C is backwards: the studio never considered this use, and IOC-1.B.4 says considering all uses is not possible. D misuses the digital divide, which concerns differing access to devices and the Internet across socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic groups (IOC-1.C.2), not professional licensing.

questions.stem: A navigation app routes each driver the fastest way. After most drivers adopt it, it sends rush-hour traffic through narrow residential streets, and neighbors petition over noise. The developers never anticipated this. Which statement BEST reflects their responsibility?
options: The developers were negligent, since responsible programmers can foresee every effect of an innovation before release
The rerouting is not a real effect of the innovation, since it happened in the physical world rather than online
Responsible programmers try to consider unintended ways their innovations can be used, but it is not possible to consider all the ways an innovation will be used or every effect in advance
An unintended effect cannot count as harmful once the developers apologize for it
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.B.3 + EK IOC-1.B.4 + EK IOC-1.A.3
questions.rationale: IOC-1.B.3 says responsible programmers try to consider unintended uses and their potential effects, IOC-1.B.4 says it is not possible to consider them all, and IOC-1.A.3 says not every effect is anticipated in advance — together they describe exactly this situation. A overstates the standard; foreseeing everything is impossible, so failing to foresee one effect is not automatic negligence. B invents a digital-only limit; effects on society (traffic, noise, safety) are the CED's central examples of impact (IOC-1.B.2). D is invented — whether an impact is harmful does not depend on intent or apology.

questions.stem: A city app lets residents report unplowed streets, and plows go where reports are highest. An audit finds higher-income neighborhoods cleared sooner: reporting needs a smartphone and data plan many lower-income residents lack. Which statement BEST connects the two ideas?
options: The crowdsourced input skews the city's decisions since the digital divide gives some groups more access and influence than others
The crowdsourcing failed since participants in crowdsourced projects must be subject-matter experts in snow removal
The audit proves the bias was written into the dispatch algorithm's code rather than arising from any gap in access
The digital divide exists only between countries, so the audit's explanation must be mistaken
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.E.4 + EK IOC-1.C.2 + EK IOC-1.C.4
questions.rationale: Synthesis of crowdsourcing and the digital divide: the app is crowdsourcing — obtaining input from a large number of people via the Internet (IOC-1.E.4) — and the digital divide is differing access to devices and the Internet based on socioeconomic characteristics (IOC-1.C.2), which raises issues of equity, access, and influence locally (IOC-1.C.4): under-connected neighborhoods generate fewer reports and so less influence. B is false — crowdsourcing draws on the general public, not experts (IOC-1.E.3 notes contributors may not even be scientists). C picks the wrong mechanism: the audit traced the skew to unequal access to devices and data plans, not to a rule in the code. D contradicts IOC-1.C.4 — the divide operates both globally AND locally.

questions.stem: An online astronomy contest is open to any student with a browser. Entries come mostly from high-income countries; urban students enter several times the rural rate; and entries drop sharply among older applicants. These patterns BEST illustrate that:
options: Interest in astronomy is the only factor that determines who enters online contests
Once a country builds Internet infrastructure, access becomes identical for everyone inside that country
Internet access varies between countries and across socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics
The contest's scoring algorithm must have contained bias inherited from its training data
questions.rationale: IOC-1.C.1 says Internet access varies between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics as well as between countries — the three observed gaps (country income, urban/rural geography, applicant age) map onto exactly those categories, and IOC-1.C.2 names this differing access the digital divide. A ignores access entirely — a browser-based contest still requires a device and connectivity. B contradicts the within-country urban/rural gap in the scenario. D reaches for computing bias (IOC-1.D.1), but the scenario describes who can enter, not how entries are scored.

questions.stem: A phone's portrait mode reliably detects lighter-skinned faces but often fails on darker-skinned faces; it was trained mostly on lighter-skinned photos. Which statement best describes the cause?
options: The phone's processor is too slow to run face detection on some photographs
The innovation reflects existing human bias through the biased data used to build it
Reliable face detection is technically impossible without violating user privacy
Bias can arise only when a developer deliberately writes an exclusionary rule into the code
questions.rationale: IOC-1.D.1 says computing innovations can reflect existing human biases because of biases written into algorithms OR biases in the data used by the innovation — a training library that underrepresents darker-skinned faces is bias in the data, and the feature inherits it. A cannot explain a failure pattern that tracks skin tone rather than image size or speed. C is an invented trade-off unrelated to the cause. D is wrong twice: bias need not be deliberate, and data is an independent source of bias alongside written rules (IOC-1.D.1, D.3).

questions.stem: A team builds a scholarship-matching tool. The lead schedules bias reviews at EVERY stage — requirements, design, data selection, testing, and post-release monitoring — instead of only when training data is chosen. Which statement BEST supports this plan?
options: Bias can enter only at the data-selection stage, so the additional reviews add cost without value
Bias reviews after release are pointless, since deployed software can no longer be changed
A tool that matches students to scholarships is beneficial, and beneficial tools cannot embed bias
Biases can be embedded at all levels of software development, and programmers should take action to reduce bias in their algorithms
questions.rationale: IOC-1.D.3 says biases can be embedded at ALL levels of software development — requirements, design, data, testing, and beyond — and IOC-1.D.2 says programmers should take action to reduce bias in algorithms as a way of combating existing human biases, which is precisely a review at every stage. A contradicts IOC-1.D.3 by shrinking bias to one stage. B is false — post-release monitoring is one of the actions programmers can take, and software is routinely updated. C is a non sequitur: an innovation's beneficial purpose does not prevent it from reflecting human bias (IOC-1.D.1).

questions.stem: Volunteers, most with no scientific training, clip lab-supplied sensors to fences and use their own phones to upload frog-call recordings for a species-decline study. Which term best describes this arrangement?
options: Citizen science — scientific research conducted in part by distributed individuals, many not scientists, who contribute data using their own computing devices
Open access — the recordings are research output free of copyright restrictions
The digital divide — participation depends on owning a backyard fence
Machine learning — the sensors discover patterns in the frog calls on their own
questions.rationale: IOC-1.E.3 defines citizen science as scientific research conducted in whole or part by distributed individuals, many of whom may not be scientists, who contribute relevant data using their own computing devices — every element matches (distributed volunteers, no training required, their own phones, data for research), and IOC-1.E.2 notes science has been affected by exactly this model. B names a publishing model (IOC-1.F.5), not a data-collection model. C misuses the digital divide, which concerns differing access to devices and the Internet (IOC-1.C.2). D describes an analysis technique, not the volunteers' role in the research.

questions.stem: A museum posts scans of 60,000 handwritten whaling-ship logbooks online. Within months, thousands of volunteers worldwide type out the weather notes, producing a searchable climate record no single research team could have created. This project BEST illustrates:
options: Plagiarism, since the volunteers transcribed material created by someone else
Targeted marketing, since the museum gathered data about its visitors' interests
Crowdsourcing — obtaining input from a large number of people via the Internet — through which collaboration enhances human capabilities
The digital divide, since volunteers in different countries typed at different speeds
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.E.4 + EK IOC-1.E.5 + EK IOC-1.E.1
questions.rationale: IOC-1.E.4 defines crowdsourcing as obtaining input or information from a large number of people via the Internet, and IOC-1.E.5 says human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing — thousands of volunteers produced a record no single team could (IOC-1.E.1: widespread access to information facilitates solutions). A is wrong — transcribing for the museum's stated project, with no one presenting the logs as their own work, is not plagiarism (IOC-1.F.4). B is unrelated; no advertising or visitor profiling occurs. D misapplies the digital divide, which is about differing access, not typing speed.

questions.stem: A podcaster finds a travel blogger's essay online, reads it aloud as his own, never asking permission or naming the blogger. Which statement best describes the ownership and his act?
options: The essay became public property when it was posted online, so the reading raises no concerns
The essay is the blogger's intellectual property; using it without permission and presenting it as his own is plagiarism, which may have legal consequences
The reading created a brand-new work, so the spoken version belongs entirely to the podcaster
The essay is jointly owned, since digital material belongs to whoever downloads it
questions.rationale: IOC-1.F.1 says material created on a computer is the intellectual property of its creator, and IOC-1.F.4 says using material created by someone else without permission and presenting it as one's own is plagiarism and may have legal consequences — the podcaster did both. A reflects the misconception that posting online forfeits ownership; ease of access actually raises IP concerns (IOC-1.F.2). C is wrong — reading a text aloud does not transfer authorship of the words. D is invented; downloading confers no ownership, and any legitimate reuse still requires citation (IOC-1.F.6).

questions.stem: A wildlife photographer wants to keep the copyright on her photo series while letting any teacher freely share the photos and build lessons on them without contacting her for individual permission. Which choice BEST fits her goal?
options: Declare the series open access, which strips research output of all restrictions on use
Keep standard full copyright, which already allows teachers to share the work freely
Label the series open source, so anyone may modify and redistribute its program code
Release the series under a Creative Commons license — a public copyright license enabling free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work
questions.rationale: IOC-1.F.5 describes Creative Commons as a public copyright license that enables free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work, used when the creator wants to give others the right to share, use, and build upon it — precisely the photographer's goal, and IOC-1.F.7 notes such licenses enable broad access. A misapplies open access, which concerns online research output, and would abandon more control than she wants. B is wrong — full copyright is exactly what forces teachers to request permission. C misapplies open source, which the CED defines for programs that are freely available to redistribute and modify, not photographs.

questions.stem: A student wants a $300 video-editing suite. A "free-downloads" site offers a cracked copy — the paid program with its licensing checks stripped out by strangers. Which TWO concerns from this course does downloading the cracked copy raise?
options: It disregards the intellectual-property protections on the creator's work, and untrustworthy free downloads can contain malware
It weakens the strength of the student's passwords, and it breaks the site's crowdsourcing agreement
It is legal since the site distributes the file publicly, though the large file may slow the computer
It qualifies as fair educational use, and files from frequently visited sites never carry malware
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.F.2 + EK IOC-1.F.3 + EK IOC-2.C.7
questions.rationale: Synthesis of legal/ethical and safe-computing concerns: the suite is its creator's intellectual property, ease of digital distribution raises concerns about its ownership, value, and use, and measures should be taken to safeguard it (IOC-1.F.1/F.2/F.3); separately, IOC-2.C.7 warns that untrustworthy (often free) downloads from freeware or shareware sites can contain malware. B invents both halves — no password mechanics or crowdsourcing agreement is involved. C confuses availability with legality; public posting does not authorize distribution of paid software. D is wrong twice: the CED offers Creative Commons, open source, and open access as the legal reuse routes (IOC-1.F.5), and no site's downloads are guaranteed malware-free.

questions.stem: A neighborhood app uses face matching on pooled doorbell video to flag 'unfamiliar people.' Residents praise fewer thefts, but delivery workers on regular routes are wrongly flagged; the model was trained on a narrow set of faces. Each flagged face is stored with its time and place. Which statements are true? I. The flagging effect can be seen as beneficial by some and harmful by others. II. The wrongful flags can reflect human bias in the training data. III. The stored face data is biometric data, a form of PII.
options: I and II only
I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.A.4 + EK IOC-1.D.1 + EK IOC-2.A.1
questions.rationale: All three are true, synthesizing three topics on one innovation. I: the same flagging effect is beneficial to residents and harmful to delivery workers — a single effect viewed both ways by different people (IOC-1.A.4). II: a matching model trained on a narrow set of faces reflects human bias through the data used by the innovation (IOC-1.D.1). III: IOC-2.A.1 lists biometric data among the examples of PII, and stored faces with time and place also add location data (IOC-2.A.4). A misses that faces are PII; B misses that unrepresentative training data is a source of bias; C misses the dual-view effect.

questions.stem: A student installs a free add-on from an unofficial forum. Weeks later, strangers log in to her accounts. Security software finds the add-on recorded every keystroke, including her passwords, and sent them to an unknown server. Which technique gained access?
options: Phishing — a counterfeit login page tricked her into submitting her credentials
A rogue access point — a wireless network intercepted the data she sent over it
Keylogging — a program recorded every keystroke she made in order to gain fraudulent access to passwords and other confidential information
A digital certificate — a certificate authority validated the attacker's ownership of her encryption keys
questions.rationale: IOC-2.C.2 defines keylogging as using a program to record every keystroke made by a computer user to gain fraudulent access to passwords and other confidential information — the add-on did exactly that, and IOC-2.C.7 explains how it arrived: untrustworthy free downloads can contain malware. A does not fit — she was never lured into typing credentials on a fake page (IOC-2.C.1); the recording happened on her own machine. B involves interception on a public network through an unauthorized access point (IOC-2.C.3/C.4), not installed software. D inverts the role of certificate authorities, which validate key ownership to SECURE communications (IOC-2.B.6).

questions.stem: At install time, a free flashlight app requests permission to access a phone's precise location, contact list, and microphone — none of which is needed to switch the LED on. What is the user's BEST protective step?
options: Review the app's permission settings and grant only the access the program actually needs, since users can control what programs may collect
Grant everything requested, since the operating system automatically blocks any misuse of granted permissions
Assume the collected information is erased automatically the moment the app is closed
Trust the app store listing, since free apps are prohibited from collecting personal data
questions.rationale: IOC-2.B.11 says users can control the permissions programs have for collecting user information and should review those permission settings to protect their privacy — an over-reaching flashlight app is the classic case. The stakes are real: programs granted location access can record where you have been, how you got there, and how long you stayed (IOC-2.A.14). B is false — granting a permission is the authorization; nothing blocks its use afterward. C contradicts IOC-2.A.13: once information is collected and placed online it is difficult to delete. D is invented — free apps commonly collect data, which is often how they are monetized (IOC-2.A.5).

partII.instructions: Answer in complete sentences. Name specific effects, attack techniques, and protections using precise course vocabulary (beneficial/harmful effect, unintended use, PII, phishing, keylogging, rogue access point, multifactor authentication, encryption).

questions.prompt: CanopyWatch is a network of solar-powered, AI-equipped mountaintop cameras that scan forests for the first wisps of wildfire smoke and automatically text evacuation alerts to residents. Its stated purpose is early wildfire detection. Within a year: fire crews credit it with saving two towns; insurers buy CanopyWatch imagery to raise premiums on homes the cameras can see; hikers use the public live feeds to find uncrowded trails; and a civil-liberties group reports the cameras also capture people and vehicles on private land, with all footage stored indefinitely by the vendor.
parts: (a) Identify ONE beneficial effect and ONE harmful effect of CanopyWatch, and then explain how a SINGLE effect from the scenario could be viewed as beneficial by one group and harmful by another.

(b) Using CanopyWatch, explain what it means for a computing innovation to have an impact beyond its intended purpose, and explain why its programmers could not have anticipated every such use in advance.
(c) Describe ONE data privacy, security, or storage concern raised by the scenario. Identify the kind of data involved and explain the risk its collection and storage creates.
sample: (a) Beneficial effect: early smoke detection and automatic evacuation alerts protect lives and property — fire crews credit the system with saving two towns (people create computing innovations to achieve such purposes, IOC-1.A.1). Harmful effect: continuous imaging of private land subjects residents to surveillance they never agreed to, a harmful impact on society (IOC-1.B.2). A single dual-view effect: the constant, always-on monitoring of the mountainsides is viewed as beneficial by residents who want the earliest possible fire warning, and as harmful by the civil-liberties group and landowners who see the same monitoring as an invasion of privacy — a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people (IOC-1.A.4).

(b) CanopyWatch was created for wildfire detection, but it is being used in ways its creators had not originally intended (IOC-1.B.1): insurers mine the imagery to raise premiums, and hikers repurpose the feeds as a trail-crowding tool — one use with a harmful economic impact on homeowners (IOC-1.B.2) and one unintended, mostly beneficial use. Responsible programmers try to consider the unintended ways their innovations can be used and the potential beneficial and harmful effects of those uses (IOC-1.B.3), but it is not possible for a programmer to consider ALL the ways an innovation can be used (IOC-1.B.4) — the developers could not reasonably have foreseen every third party, like insurers, who would buy imagery once the feeds existed, and not every effect of an innovation is anticipated in advance (IOC-1.A.3).

(c) The cameras collect images of people and vehicles on private land and store the footage indefinitely. Vehicle plates, faces, and repeated appearances at one property are information that identifies, links, relates, or describes individuals — PII (IOC-2.A.1) — and the system effectively records location data about where people are and when (IOC-2.A.4, IOC-2.A.14). The risk: disparate data like this can be aggregated to create knowledge about an individual (IOC-2.A.7), such as daily routines revealing when a home is empty; PII can be used to stalk or steal the identity of a person or to aid in planning other criminal acts (IOC-2.A.12); commercial curation of the footage — like the insurance sales — can be exploited when privacy protections are ignored (IOC-2.A.10); and once such information is collected and placed online it is difficult to delete (IOC-2.A.13).
rubric: 1 pt: Part (a) identifies a plausible beneficial effect tied to the scenario (early detection/alerts saving lives or property).
1 pt: Part (a) identifies a plausible harmful effect tied to the scenario (surveillance of private land, premium hikes) (IOC-1.B.2).
1 pt: Part (a) explains ONE single effect viewed as beneficial by one group and harmful by another (IOC-1.A.4).
1 pt: Part (b) identifies a use beyond the intended purpose from the scenario (insurers or hikers) (IOC-1.B.1).
1 pt: Part (b) states that responsible programmers try to consider unintended uses and effects (IOC-1.B.3).
1 pt: Part (b) states that it is not possible to consider all uses / not every effect is anticipated in advance (IOC-1.B.4/IOC-1.A.3).
1 pt: Part (c) identifies the collected data and classifies it (PII and/or location data) (IOC-2.A.1/A.4/A.14).
1 pt: Part (c) explains a concrete risk of collection/aggregation (stalking, criminal planning, exploitation of curated data) (IOC-2.A.7/A.12/A.10).
1 pt: Part (c) addresses storage — indefinite retention and the difficulty of deleting information once collected (IOC-2.A.13).
questions.ek: EK IOC-1.A.3 + EK IOC-1.A.4 + EK IOC-1.B.1 + EK IOC-1.B.2 + EK IOC-1.B.3 + EK IOC-1.B.4 + EK IOC-2.A.1 + EK IOC-2.A.4 + EK IOC-2.A.7 + EK IOC-2.A.12 + EK IOC-2.A.13

questions.prompt: At a robotics tournament, three students on one team are compromised. INCIDENT 1: The captain gets an email styled exactly like the organizer's, warning that her match schedule "contains an error" and that she must verify her account within one hour at a linked page. The page looks identical to the real portal but sits at a slightly different web address; she enters her username and password. INCIDENT 2: A teammate installed a free "battery optimizer" from a shareware site on the team laptop; unknown to him, it records every keystroke — including the team's cloud-storage password — and sends the logs to an attacker. INCIDENT 3: A third student joins a wireless network named "ConventionCenter_Free" that is actually broadcast from a backpack in the bleachers, and everything she transmits over it is intercepted and read.
parts: (a) For EACH of the three incidents, name the attack technique being used and cite the detail from the scenario that identifies it.

(b) The stolen passwords from Incidents 1 and 2 let the attacker into both accounts. Explain how multifactor authentication would have prevented these account takeovers even after the passwords were captured. Name the categories of evidence MFA can combine.

(c) Explain how encryption protects data sent over a public network like the one in Incident 3, distinguishing symmetric key from public key encryption, and state the role of certificate authorities. Then recommend ONE additional protection that addresses Incident 2 and justify it.
sample: (a) Incident 1 is phishing — a technique that attempts to trick a user into providing personal information that can then be used to access sensitive online resources (IOC-2.C.1). The identifying details: an unsolicited urgent email with a link (IOC-2.C.6), a disguised malicious link styled as the organizer's portal (IOC-2.C.5), and a look-alike page at a slightly different address where she typed her credentials. Incident 2 is keylogging — the use of a program to record every keystroke made by the user in order to gain fraudulent access to passwords and other confidential information (IOC-2.C.2); it arrived through an untrustworthy free download from a shareware site, which can contain malware (IOC-2.C.7). Incident 3 is a rogue access point — a wireless access point that gives unauthorized access to secure networks (IOC-2.C.4); the fake "ConventionCenter_Free" network shows that data sent over public networks can be intercepted, analyzed, and modified (IOC-2.C.3).

(b) Authentication measures protect devices and information from unauthorized access (IOC-2.B.1). Multifactor authentication grants access only after the user presents several separate pieces of evidence, typically from at least two of three categories: knowledge (something they know), possession (something they have), and inherence (something they are) (IOC-2.B.3). In both incidents the attacker captured only the knowledge factor — a password. With MFA enabled, logging in would also require a second step, such as a code on the captain's phone (possession) or a fingerprint (inherence). MFA requires at least two steps to unlock protected information, and each step adds a new layer of security that must be broken to gain unauthorized access (IOC-2.B.4) — so the stolen passwords alone would not have opened either account.

(c) Encryption is the process of encoding data to prevent unauthorized access, and decryption is the process of decoding it (IOC-2.B.5). If the third student's traffic had been encrypted, the rogue access point could still intercept the packets but could not read them. Symmetric key encryption uses ONE shared key for both encryption and decryption, while public key encryption pairs a public key for encryption with a private key for decryption — the sender does not need the receiver's private key to encrypt, but only the receiver's private key can decrypt (IOC-2.B.5), which is why strangers on a public network can still communicate securely. Certificate authorities issue digital certificates that validate the ownership of the encryption keys used in secure communications, based on a trust model (IOC-2.B.6) — that validation is what tells a browser it is encrypting to the real portal rather than to the attacker in the bleachers. For Incident 2: install virus and malware scanning software, which can help protect a computing system against infection (IOC-2.B.7) and could have caught the keylogger; keeping software regularly updated also helps, since all real-world systems have errors or design flaws that updates fix before they can be exploited (IOC-2.B.10).
rubric: 1 pt: Part (a) names phishing for Incident 1 with an identifying detail (deceptive email/disguised link/look-alike page) (IOC-2.C.1/C.5/C.6).
1 pt: Part (a) names keylogging for Incident 2 with the keystroke-recording detail, and ties its arrival to the untrustworthy free download (IOC-2.C.2/C.7).
1 pt: Part (a) names a rogue access point for Incident 3 and notes that data on public networks can be intercepted (IOC-2.C.3/C.4).
1 pt: Part (b) states that MFA requires several separate pieces of evidence across categories (IOC-2.B.3).
1 pt: Part (b) names the three categories: knowledge, possession, inherence (IOC-2.B.3).
1 pt: Part (b) explains why the stolen password alone fails — each MFA step is an added security layer that must also be broken (IOC-2.B.4).
1 pt: Part (c) defines encryption/decryption and applies it to the intercepted traffic (IOC-2.B.5).

1 pt: Part (c) correctly distinguishes symmetric (one shared key) from public key encryption (public key encrypts, private key decrypts) and states the certificate authority's validation role (IOC-2.B.5/B.6).
1 pt: Part (c) recommends and justifies malware-scanning software and/or regular updates for Incident 2 (IOC-2.B.7/B.10).
questions.ek: EK IOC-2.C.1 + EK IOC-2.C.2 + EK IOC-2.C.3 + EK IOC-2.C.4 + EK IOC-2.C.5 + EK IOC-2.C.6 + EK IOC-2.C.7 + EK IOC-2.B.1 + EK IOC-2.B.3 + EK IOC-2.B.4 + EK IOC-2.B.5 + EK IOC-2.B.6 + EK IOC-2.B.7 + EK IOC-2.B.10
